Two Giant Benchmark Funds: IEMG and VWO

The largest EM fund is the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (IEMG), it has a Z-Score of (-0.03,-0.20), which means it has almost exactly average performance. The top holding is TSMC (11.41%) followed by Samsung (5.29%) Tencent Holdings Limited (TCEHY) (3.14%) and SK hynix Inc. (HXSC.F) (3.01%). Its vast 2690 holdings feature a wide range of industries, with Tech being about one-third. It has essentially performed at the EM average because it owns the whole the market. If you own IEMG you have seen a 32.83% total return in the last 12 months, so although it’s only an average performer it has still given you good gains because the Emerging Market has been so strong. IEMG has a Seeking Alpha Quant rating of Buy. If you hold it then you own the whole Emerging Market as closely as possible. It acts as a geographic diversifier, but not a sector diversifier if you already own Tech and particularly semiconductor stocks. Moreover, any buyer should realize that 11.41% of the ETF is a stock you can easily buy directly on the NYSE.

The only other fund that has AUM comparable to IEMG is the Vanguard Emerging Markets Stock Index Fund ETF (VWO). This has an enormous 5029 holdings, so you might also expect it to have an average Z-Score, because it owns the whole market, but its actual Z-Score is (-0.80,0.86). The difference is primarily down to the absence of South Korea in the index because VWO is FTSE benchmarked and they promoted South Korea to developed in 2009, whereas the MSCI (used for IEMG) still has South Korea as Emerging. No Korea means there’s no Samsung and SK Hynix in VWO at all, although it still does have TSMC (11.72%) and Tencent (4.15%) as its biggest holdings. The weighting of Tech is significantly lower than IEMG at 23.66%. This leads to a Z-Score of (-0.80,0.86), showing underperformance in the 12 month rising market and outperformance in the sharp drawdown. It has provided a reasonable 22.45% return over 1 year, significantly lower than IEMG, but has shown greater resilience to the shock because it isn’t exposed to the concentration risk in South Korea. It is effectively a reduced volatility EM ETF when Korea is so volatile, but it’s worth noting that the absence of Korea means China has a much higher weighting. The Seeking Alpha Quant rating for VWO is Hold, which is the worst on our shortlist. It mostly suffers from a poor momentum score as it is lagging due to not holding South Korea. It is an interesting quirk of the benchmarking that you can hold VWO to get exposure to Emerging Markets but avoid the South Korea concentration risk and diversify somewhat away from semiconductors. However, realise that if the AI chip boom continues, VWO will significantly underperform.

Optimized Proxy Benchmark: JEMA

The only non-specialised ETF with a Z-score in the upper-right quadrant (+,+) is JEMA, with a Z-Score of (0.42,0.26). It is an outperformer on both timeframes and is essentially slightly above average in everything, which is a rarity in EM ETFs, as our Z-Scores show. It’s not immediately obvious how JEMA outperforms the average when looking at its top holdings. It is heavily into Tech (35.37%) and has a significant South Korea element (17.4%), which is virtually identical to that of IEMG, as well as the same top 4 holdings as IEMG with slightly different weightings: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) (13.03%), Samsung (7.45%), Tencent (4.22%) and SK hynix (3.50%). A key aspect is that it is an actively managed fund, and a notable difference in its main holdings compared to the benchmark is a significant allocation to cash and equivalents (2.08%). This will have certainly helped cushion the downswing, but it also means the portfolio managers have the ability to buy the dip, something a passive benchmark cannot do. Given that the major holdings are very similar to IEMG, it is clear that the portfolio managers have a quality screening that has removed a significant number of small allocations to underperformers compared to the passive indices (it has 539 holdings vs IEMG’s 2690). The expense ratio of JEMA (0.34%) is significantly higher than IEMG (0.09%), but given that the Total Return used to construct the Z-Scores includes this expense, it is clearly justifying it as it outperforms on both growth and resilience despite the higher fee. JEMA also comes with a 2.67% dividend, which is about average for these EM ETFs.

The Seeking Alpha Quant score is a solid Buy with consistent scores across all categories. It is recommended to buy JEMA to own the broad emerging market (including Korea) with a heavy Tech component while severing a 2000+ stock tail of small allocations that appear to be acting as a drag on the passive benchmark. Although the two giants provide two distinct options (with or without Korea), JEMA includes Korea for its incredible growth, but manages to increase the resilience through its actively managed stock-picking. It beats IEMG on both Z-Scores, and although VWO beats JEMA for resilience, it dramatically underperforms on growth (which is where the big gains are made). The Z-Score map tells the story for JEMA, because it is one of only three ETFs in the (+,+) quadrant (the other two being very specific thematic ETFs). If you only buy one EM ETF and want broad-market coverage, JEMA is the clear winner over the two giants.

Conclusion and Outlook

In this article we have seen that IEMG buys the whole Emerging Market and provided average returns during both the 1-year growth phase and the rapid geopolitically induced drop. It is exactly what you would expect from a benchmark. VWO, by a quirk of the benchmarking criteria, excludes South Korea and therefore provides a very different return: significantly underperforming during the growth phase, but being much more resilient during the turmoil. This difference demonstrates the oversized influence of Korea on the emerging market.

Our Z-Score analysis identified JEMA as an optimized proxy for the EM benchmark that outperforms on both growth and resilience. It is a much smaller fund in terms of AUM, but it is still diverse in terms of holdings, with broad sector and geographical exposure (and it does hold Korea). Its outperformance is attributable to an actively managed top-down and bottom up approach to portfolio construction, in particular a concentrated tilt towards quality has seen a long tail of 2000+ holdings shed in comparison to the benchmarks. Looking ahead, the ability to shift allocations is seen as a significant edge as news and macro is whipped around by geopolitics, especially because some of the regions in the emerging market are particularly vulnerable to energy shocks. Weightings can be shifted from vulnerable energy importers like South Korea to oil producing countries like Brazil to capitalze on this instability, or vice versa to buy-the-dip on growth when deemed appropriate. Whether this will greatly benefit the fund in the future is uncertain as it relies on the ability of the fund managers. However, they can only be judged on their track record and our Z-Score analysis demonstrates that they have recently outperformed the passive benchmark on both growth and resilience.

If the managers can amplify trends and trend reversals with their stock-picking, then JEMA will continue to outperform, maybe even more so as the fastest money is made at inflection points. At the very least, JEMA’s risk-adjusted floor is superior to the passive funds, due to the more focused portfolio. With JEMA, instead of owning the whole emerging market, you can own an optimized emerging market.

The 40 ETFs used in the Z-Score analysis are (in alphabetical order): the abrdn Emerging Markets ex-China Fund, Inc. (AEF), the Avantis Emerging Markets Equity ETF (AVEM), the JPMorgan BetaBuilders Emerging Markets Equity ETF (BBEM), WisdomTree Emerging Markets High Dividend Fund ETF (DEM), the DFA Dimensional Emerging Core Equity Market ETF (DFAE), the DFA Dimensional Emerging Markets Value ETF (DFEV), the WisdomTree Emerging Markets Quality Dividend Gro ETF (DGRE), the WisdomTree Emerging Markets SmallCap Dividend Fund ETF (DGS), the iShares Emerging Markets Dividend ETF (DVYE), the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (EEM), the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets Asia ETF (EEMA), the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets Small-Cap ETF (EEMS), the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets Min Vol Factor ETF (EEMV), EMEQ, the iShares Emerging Markets Equity Factor ETF (EMGF), the iShares Emerg Markets Infrastructure ETF (EMIF), the AB Emerging Markets Opportunities ETF (EMOP), the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets ex China ETF (EMXC), the iShares ESG Advanced MSCI EM ETF (EMXF), the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets Quality Factor ETF (EQLT), the the iShares ESG Aware MSCI EM ETF (ESGE), the iShares MSCI Emerging Markets Value Factor ETF (EVLU), the State Street SPDR S&P Emerging Mkts Small Cap ETF (EWX), the Cambria Emerging Shareholder Yield ETF (EYLD), the Schwab Fundamental EM Equity ETF (FNDE), the Freedom 100 Emerging Markets ETF (FRDM), the Goldman Sachs ActiveBeta Em Mkts Equity ETF (GEM), the State Street SPDR S&P Emerging Asia Pacific ETF (GMF), IEMG, JEMA, the JPMorgan Diversified Return Emerging Mkts Eqty ETF (JPEM), the iShares ESG MSCI EM Leaders ETF (LDEM), the Invesco RAFI Emerging Markets ETF (PXH), the Schwab Emerging Markets Equity ETF (SCHE), the SEI Select Emerging Markets Equity ETF (SEEM), the State Street SPDR Portfolio Emerging Markets ETF (SPEM), the Vanguard Emerging Markets Ex-China ETF (VEXC), VWO, the Columbia EM Core ex-China ETF (XCEM), and the WisdomTree EM ex-State-Owned Enterprises ETF (XSOE). All data is taken from Seeking Alpha unless stated otherwise.

data.title: Amazon: Positioning For Global Interconnectivity With Globalstar
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) recently announced the acquisition of Globalstar, Inc. (GSAT) in a deal valuing GSAT shares at roughly $11.5b at $90/share, valuing shares at 84.42x EV/aEBITDA. With a growing need for connectivity, I believe the Globalstar acquisition goes beyond the headline direct-to-device pitch that’s been circulating the media.

Diving deeper, Globalstar has been developing two-way connectivity infrastructure for industrial IoT, an appealing system for active and continuous monitoring as well as overriding devices remotely. With Amazon aiming to automate more warehouse operations as well as the possibility of automating last-mile delivery services, Globalstar’s technology may be an ideal fit for completing the interconnected network from a centralized data center site.

Amazon's Acquisition Of Globalstar

Globalstar shareholders will have the option to elect to either receive $90/share in cash or 0.3210 shares of Amazon stock at a $90/share price cap. With the transaction expected to close in 2027, investors are showing signs of early skepticism of the deal going through given the GSAT share price dispersion from the acquisition price. The way to think about this is that the further the share price deviates from the acquisition price, the less optimistic investors are becoming regarding the ability to complete the acquisition. While a 12% dispersion isn’t substantial, it is wide enough to suggest some skepticism.

The acquisition comes at a time of growing interest in space exploration and technology, with the recently announced plans to bring SpaceX public, Artemis II doing a lunar flyby, and the initial developments of the Golden Dome taking place. While Amazon has been in the works in expanding its Blue Origin satellite constellation for broadband connectivity, the Globalstar acquisition may provide the company with a differentiated spin that appears to be overlooked by the analyst community.

What’s being ignored by the headlines is Globalstar’s expertise in industrial IoT two-way connectivity. While analysts are focused on spectrum and direct-to-device (D2D) connectivity, which is a critical component to the deal, I believe the two-way connectivity component is just as critical, particularly when considering Amazon’s transition to more warehouse automation.

In late 2025, Amazon announced a major robotics initiative, Blue Jay, which was purpose-built for same-day delivery warehouse operations. The program turned out to be overly complex as well as costly and was later put on hold in February 2026. What may have been missing from the mix was the level of connectivity provided by Globalstar’s two-way IoT communications satellites, which allow data to be transmitted both to and from the infrastructure for continuous monitoring.

The press release issued by Amazon regarding the acquisition focused mostly on D2D capabilities enabled by Globalstar’s satellite constellation, which very well can be the ultimate strategy for Amazon. Accordingly, Globalstar’s D2D infrastructure enables voice, text, and data services all connected and transmitted through its satellites. As part of the deal, Amazon entered into an agreement with Apple (AAPL) as a continuation of Globalstar’s partnership with the iPhone maker, which will transition Apple’s services to Amazon Leo to enable direct-to-mobile capabilities.

While this can be an appealing vision, I suspect that Amazon will benefit most from the Commercial IoT business, which connects industrial sites for remote monitoring. This can be a critical feature depending on Amazon’s future plans for data center developments and automated warehouses, which could potentially be developed in less populated regions and only supported remotely. This technology could also be leveraged to create a direct link between Amazon’s data centers and its warehouses for continuous monitoring, potentially unlocking capabilities that were limited to regional infrastructure capabilities.

This could also be a long-term strategy for the future of Amazon’s final-mile deliveries, essentially extending Amazon’s connectivity to every region covered by the satellites. This may mean that Amazon will be capable of orchestrating an entire fleet of autonomous delivery vans from a central data center, controlling the process from warehouse to doorstep through one connected system. Granted, this may be a few years out, as a mobile robot will be necessary to transport the box from the van to the door, but this may be the ultimate goal of the Globalstar acquisition.

In terms of the financial impact on Amazon, Globalstar isn’t going to provide any meaningful impact on operations; in the last twelve months, Globalstar reported $136mm in adjusted EBITDA, far overshadowed by Amazon’s $169b. The acquisition is expected to be more tied to access to the technology than anything else.

Other Operational Updates

The Wall Street Journal recently reported a timely article regarding the state of the AI data center marketplace, suggesting a tight demand market as the supply of compute capacity remains a constraint. As a result of this, pricing for access to cloud-hosted GPUs has been increasing across multiple hosts, including CoreWeave (CRWV). This could potentially mean that Amazon may realize improved economics for its cloud hosting services over the coming quarters, resulting in a return to strengthening margins.

Amazon Financial Position & Forecast

Looking to the Q1 ’26 release on April 29th, I’m maintaining my previous forecast for Amazon with $178b in net revenue with a diluted EPS of $1.72/share. Given the recent report in the WSJ, I’m expecting the remainder of eFY26 to realize incremental improvements to AWS margins, creating an uplift in profitability while partially offsetting the higher depreciation expenses tied to compute capacity.

In terms of the financial impact from the Globalstar acquisition, if the deal goes through, I believe Amazon may unlock the technology necessary to automate a greater deal of its operational footprint, potentially creating an environment that can realize a substantial uptick in margins once fully integrated. While I don’t expect this to have an immediate impact, this should work into cost management efforts at the turn of the decade.

Risks Related To Amazon & Globalstar

Bull Case

Amazon remains well positioned with dominance in the cloud services business, with demand outstripping supply. Given the potential for improved pricing for hosted GPUs, Amazon may realize incremental improvements to AWS margins in the coming quarters.

An acquisition of Globalstar may accelerate Amazon’s autonomous operational ambitions across its warehouses and final-mile delivery services, potentially creating an interconnected network across the complete supply chain.

Bear Case

The market is pricing uncertainty regarding Amazon’s ability to close the deal with Globalstar. The acquisition is relatively expensive and may provide limited operational upside potential for Amazon if technology development doesn’t materialize.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

AMZN shares are currently trading at an appealing discount to their historical trading range at 15.16x EV/EBITDA, creating an opportunity for investors to build up their positions. Given the long-term growth opportunities presented through the acquisition of Globalstar, Amazon may be in a position to accelerate the development of autonomous operations through physical AI. Given the appealing foundation, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating for AMZN shares with a price target of $310/share at 17.20x eFY27 EV/aEBITDA.

As for positioning in GSAT, investors can implement an appealing merger-arbitrage strategy given the price dispersion between the offer price and the current price level. This will yield roughly 12% in gains, assuming the deal goes through and if shareholders elect to receive cash payment.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market capitalization and relative multiple.

data.title: MFA Financial: 10% Yielding Preferred Shares Are A Hedge Against Inflation
data.description:
Introduction

MFA Financial (MFA) is a real estate investment trust that specializes in the investment of mortgages, also known as an mREIT. Like other mREITs, MFA offers a high-yielding dividend on its common shares and has two preferred shares and two baby bonds (MFAN) and (MFAO). The two preferred shares have two different payment structures, with one offering a fixed payment (MFA.PB) currently yielding 9.33% and a floating payment (MFA.PC) currently yielding just over 10%.

Back in September, I pointed to the premium yield in the Series C preferred shares as a reason for income investors to put their money there. Today, the yields are comparatively the same, but with the threat of interest rates rising due to recent inflation shocks, the Series C preferred shares could offer income investors a good hedge against inflation risks.

MFA Financial Earnings

Recent declines in interest rates have yet to benefit MFA Financial. The company’s yield on assets declined by 30 basis points in the fourth quarter to 6.20% and was down 44 basis points from a year ago. Borrowing yields, or cost of funds, also dropped but by less than asset yields, at 24 basis points on the quarter to 5.05%. The average cost of funds is 55 basis points higher than a year ago, reflecting that the company’s need for additional financing has come at a price.

MFA Financial has clearly taken on leverage in the pursuit of margins, and that is best expressed by looking at both the net interest spread and margin. In the fourth quarter, the net interest spread fell to 1.15%. This is a nearly 100 basis point decline from a year ago. When incorporating leverage, net interest margin’s decline is a little less dramatic, although it did decline by 26 basis points in the fourth quarter to 2.31%.

Investors should not be surprised that these trends are leading to an impact on net interest income. Interest income rose to $190 million in the fourth quarter, which is slightly higher than many of the previous quarters. Unfortunately, interest expense also rose, and by greater than interest income, at $6 million to $135 million. This led to a net interest income (interest income less interest expense) decline to $55 million, the lowest quarterly level in 2025. While many other financial institutions are seeing higher net interest income as short-term rates fall, MFA Financial’s pursuit of growth is causing the opposite effect.

Leverage is the Culprit of Headwinds

A look at the balance sheet shows the reason for many of these headwinds is not only related to the extra leverage that MFA Financial took on but also where they invested those proceeds. Debt in the form of financing agreements rose by nearly $1.8 billion during 2025, but the company’s residential whole loans were essentially flat at $8.81 billion. MFA Financial used the debt proceeds to invest in securities, growing that position from $1.5 billion to $3.36 billion. While the spread between securities and borrowing rates is narrower, there is clearly an arbitrage at play that MFA Financial wants to take advantage of.

One important metric that preferred shareholders should also keep an eye on is recourse leverage. This ratio measures the company’s leverage against assets that are collateralized (versus unsecured). Growth in this ratio could impair the fair market value of both the preferred securities and baby bonds (which are seen as a form of unsecured debt). The recourse leverage ratio rose to 2.5 to 1 in the fourth quarter, the highest level since the start of the pandemic, but investors should note the ratio is still lower than it was in 2019.

Risks to MFA Financial

One risk area for MFA Financial is the performance of its loans. High loan delinquencies can further pressure earnings spread and create volatility. I’ve been critical of many mREITs in recent months for their exposure to the private credit markets, but I don’t think that is an issue here. MFA Financial invests in residential whole loans, which seem to be stable relative to loans on commercial buildings. The company’s 60-day delinquency rate backs this theory, as it has been relatively steady around 7% over the last couple of years.

One area that investors need to watch carefully is the interest rate spreads. MFA Financial is engaged in short-term lending and is investing those funds in longer-term securities, which are likely priced at fixed rates. Should short-term rates change, MFA Financial could find itself in an inverted yield situation and see further declines in net interest income. Should this happen, I believe the common shares will take the brunt of the volatility, as that is the area where the company has struggled to create value for several years.

Conclusion

Despite the headwinds facing MFA Financial, I believe the company’s stable loan performance will guide it through these challenges. I’m not secure on holding common shares and believe they will be impacted by some of the challenges mentioned, but since common dividends must go to zero before preferred dividends are impacted, I believe an adequate buffer is in place to protect Series C shareholders and their 10% yielding dividends.

data.title: ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Strategy Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
By Erica Furfaro and Margaret Vitrano

Stabilizing Growth Setup Through Volatility

Market Overview

The outbreak of war in the Middle East accelerated a selloff among growth stocks that started with software weakness and resulted in wide losses for the first quarter. The S&P 500 Index declined 4.3% while large cap growth stocks were the worst-performing segment of the market, with the benchmark Russell 1000 Growth Index falling 9.8% compared to a gain of 2.1% for the Russell 1000 Value Index.

Growth stocks were pressured as investors more pointedly questioned the return on investment from massive capital spending on AI buildouts as well as the viability of application software business models amid new generative AI tools introduced during the quarter. Rising bond yields resulting from the inflationary pressures of higher oil prices due to the U.S. and Israeli conflict with Iran also weighed on higher-multiple growth stocks.

Against this volatile backdrop, the ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Strategy outperformed its benchmark as we saw increased market dispersion away from the technology sector and momentum stocks that led the market in 2025. Materials was the largest contributor to relative performance in the quarter as industrial gases provider Linde (LIN) and paint and coatings maker Sherwin-Williams were solid performers in an environment that has begun to reward companies with quality fundamentals.

The Strategy also saw contributions from a diversified mix of industrials names, led by Eaton (ETN), a key provider of equipment to enable electrical connectivity; W.W. Grainger (GWW), a distributor of industrial supplies; and RTX (RTX), a defense contractor. We were also encouraged by the rebound in Netflix (NFLX) after the streaming provider withdrew from a bidding war for Warner Bros. Discovery (WBD); we believe the fundamental setup for its business remains robust.

These holdings helped offset weakness among the portfolio’s software names. Tax prep software maker Intuit (INTU), credit score provider Fair Isaac (FICO), cloud hyperscaler Oracle (ORCL) and enterprise software firm Salesforce (CRM) were all impacted by a broad acceleration in the selloff among software companies feared to be disintermediated by AI. Fair Isaac, known for its FICO score, was also hurt by concerns about growing competition that we believe are overblown as the company continues to deliver considerable value to its customers. We completed an exit from Salesforce this quarter after trimming the position as part of our actions to reduce software exposure over the past year.

Since 2022, Salesforce has experienced slowing growth in its core business across its sales and service cloud segments and, more recently, marketing cloud. While Salesforce is executing on several product strategies to accelerate growth, we think these initiatives are not substantial enough to bend the curve on overall revenue growth. Product execution on innovation strategies has also been mixed. We believe Salesforce will continue to face rising competitive and model transition pressures as the software-as-a-service ecosystem moves from seat-based to usage-based pricing.

Portfolio Positioning

We continue to be optimistic on trends in the semiconductor sector and added a new position during the quarter in Texas Instruments (TXN), a company focused on analog semiconductor devices and embedded processing. The company sells products with long life cycles to a diversified customer base and has a unique free cash flow story. While the company’s cash flow has been depressed by sluggish demand and elevated capex, we believe cash flow can inflect meaningfully as both of those trends reverse. We also view Texas Instruments as well positioned for an improving manufacturing outlook as 30%–40% of its customer base is in industrials (Exhibit 1).

Exhibit 1: Sector Positioning vs Benchmark

We also added two new positions in the health care sector with the additions of Roche (RHHBY) and Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY). These moves brought the portfolio’s health care exposure back to a slight overweight versus the benchmark and continued our efforts to improve quality of our health care holdings. We believe Roche has an attractive growth runway with multiple positive Phase III readouts in early-stage breast cancer and multiple sclerosis. These should enable Roche to grow revenues through the loss of exclusivity period for several of its current cancer and multiple sclerosis drugs and secure its place among pharmaceutical companies that can grow revenue for the next decade.

Alnylam has pioneered RNAi drugs that work to "silence" certain proteins created by the body that can malfunction and cause disease. The company’s key product, Amvuttra, treats a condition that leads to protein misfolding in the heart and, eventually, heart failure (cardiomyopathy). While Amvuttra entered the cardiomyopathy market to strong early success in 2025, missed expectations heading into 2026 provided a chance to buy the stock at valuation levels we think incorporate conservative assumptions for its commercial opportunity and very little value for any other pipeline drugs. Alnylam also reached free cash flow breakeven in 2025, a milestone that we seek in biotech investments to derisk some downside potential.

We exited PayPal (PYPL) and Equinix (EQIX) during the quarter. When we invest in businesses like PayPal executing upon turnarounds, we regularly weigh the positive outcome of improved execution with downside risks, including the opportunity cost of longer than expected paths to improvement. While there are some signs of improvement in PayPal’s business, the turnaround has proved more challenging than expected, leading us to exit the position.

Equinix owns and operates interconnected data centers that provide network and cloud platforms for enterprise customers. The stock was sold as the company remains in the middle of a large capex investment cycle while AI tailwinds from increased customer spending on inference will take time to show through.

Outlook

We believe the start of 2026 demonstrated stabilization for the Strategy compared to the prior year. While the top-heavy composition of the Russell 1000 Growth Index causes us to maintain a significant weighting to the Magnificent Seven, our mega cap exposure did not have an overriding influence on first-quarter performance. Instead, we were encouraged by the diversified contributions the Strategy received across multiple sectors outside IT.

Rising geopolitical risks also highlighted our ability as active managers to oversee the level of risk in the portfolio. As market volatility rose during the quarter with the Iran conflict, we continued to dial back risk by trimming strong recent performers to redeploy the proceeds into more attractive ideas. Looking ahead, some early signs of positive early-cycle momentum in industrials have eroded somewhat due to the conflict, yet we remain optimistic that pent-up activity in the manufacturing economy can be supportive of portfolio results.

Portfolio Highlights

The ClearBridge Large Cap Growth Strategy outperformed its Russell 1000 Growth Index benchmark in the first quarter. On an absolute basis, the Strategy delivered positive contributions across two of the nine sectors in which it was invested (out of 11 total). The primary contributor was the materials sector, while the IT and consumer discretionary sectors were the main detractors.

Relative to the benchmark, overall sector allocation contributed to performance. In particular, overweights to the industrials and materials sectors and stock selection in the communication services and materials sectors supported performance. Conversely, stock selection in industrials and health care detracted as did an underweight to consumer staples.

On an individual stock basis, the leading contributors to relative performance included ASML (ASML), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), Eaton, Netflix and an underweight to Microsoft (MSFT). The primary detractors from relative returns were Intuit and Fair Isaac as well as not holding Costco Wholesale (COST), GE Vernova (GEV) and Lam Research (LRCX).

In addition to the transactions mentioned above, we initiated a position in Blackstone (BX) in the financials sector and exited a position in Marvell Technology (MRVL) in the IT sector.

Erica Furfaro, Portfolio Manager

Margaret Vitrano, Portfolio Manager

data.title: AMASS Brands Aims For Direct Listing Despite Weak Financial Results
data.description:
AMASS Brands Is A Company In Transition

AMASS Brands Inc. (AMSS) has filed for a direct listing of its common stock, according to an S-1 registration statement.

The company markets bulk wine and spirits in the United States.

While management is moving to rationalize its portfolio, the industry is suffering from declining usage from younger demographics, the company has little liquidity, and the listing will provide no new resources.

Since the only form of meaningful new capital is potentially increasingly dilutive convertible financing, my outlook on the direct listing is to sell (avoid it).

What Does AMASS Do?

AMASS sells bulk wine through distributors and also increasingly offers non-alcoholic alternatives and spirits.

It also sells directly to the consumer and through international distributors through third-party partners.

The company's portfolio of brand categories includes:

  • AMASS - flagship spirits and non-alcoholic products.

  • Spirits - GEM&BOLT Mezcal, Calirosa Tequila.

  • Wine - Summer Water, Folly of the Beast and premium imports.

  • Non-alcoholic Wine - Good Twin.

  • Minority Interests - De Soi (non-alcoholic) and HpO (protein sparkling water).

A breakdown of the firm’s revenue contribution by segment is shown in the graphic below:

AMASS is led by founder, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer Mark T. Lynn, who founded the company in 2019.

Mr. Lynn has been a director on the Board of Digital Brands Group (DBGI) and has extensive experience in the beverage and consumer industries.

Investors have invested fair market value of approximately $37.6 million to-date and include MVL, Streeterville Capital and individuals.

What Is The Primary Market For AMASS?

AMASS operates primarily in the U.S. wine market, where most of its revenues are derived.

According to a 2026 market research report by Grand View Research, the U.S. wine market was an estimated $75.3 billion in 2024 and was expected to exceed $157 billion by 2033.

If achieved, this would represent a Compound Annual Growth Rate [CAGR] of 8.8% from 2025 to 2033, a reasonably robust growth rate for an already large market and over a significant number of years.

The table wine product type accounted for 81.5% of the total market share in the U.S. in 2024, with the retail (off-trade) segment leading by distribution channel, at 76.6% of the market in 2024.

The chart below shows the historical and projected future growth path of the U.S. wine market by product type, from 2023 to 2033:

The U.S. wine market is fragmented, but major competitors include:

  • E. & J. Gallo Winery.

  • Constellation Brands (STZ).

  • The Wine Group.

  • Trinchero Family Estates.

  • Pernod Ricard (PDRDF).

  • Deutsch Family Wine & Spirits.

  • Accolade Wines.

  • Castel Frères.

  • Casella Family Brands.

  • Bronco Wine Company.

  • Numerous others.

Niche markets are gaining share among discerning consumers, as evolving tastes continue to shape the U.S. wine industry.

Consumers want diverse wine experiences, valuing "authenticity, sustainability…driving demand for wines that align with these preferences."

However, younger demographics are drinking less alcohol and participation rates are declining, according to a 2025 Gallup study.

Younger demographic cohorts, such as younger millennials and older Gen Z persons, have a growing belief that moderate alcohol consumption is bad for their health, resulting in a record low 54% of Americans saying they consume alcohol.

AMASS’s strengths include its multi-category portfolio, design-led branding, alignment with younger and moderation-oriented buyers, and celebrity partnerships (Katy Perry, Adam Levine).

The company's weak points are execution strain across its various brands, reducing focus on some of its spirits in the short term, using direct-to-consumer for only brand support channel.

Opportunities for AMSS are through focusing on existing wholesalers for deeper distribution penetration, new product launches in the moderation and wellness segments, and cross-selling and portfolio bundling.

The firm is seeing threats due to demand softness in US spirits, large incumbent competitiveness and regulatory complexity.

Recent Financial Results For AMASS

AMASS’s financial results have produced falling topline revenue, higher gross profit and gross margin, worse operating losses but reduced cash flow from operations, as the table shows below:

AMASS’s Direct Listing Plan

AMSS is registering for sale up to 12,432,021 shares of common stock held by shareholders.

In the prospectus, the company cites an Expected Reference Price of $16.00 per share, which if confirmed by the firm’s Advisor on the day of listing, would mean an initial market capitalization of approximately $199 million.

Certain shareholders have agreed to a tiered lock-up period, subject to certain exceptions.

Management intends to host an "investor day," with content similar to that of a traditional IPO roadshow presentation.

The company will receive no proceeds from the direct listing process, so it will have no additional financial resources with which to further its position in the markets it operates in.

The firm said it is not currently a party to any legal claims or proceedings that it considers material.

However, it is involved in a legal action against a former landlord in a lease termination case. The parties have entered into a termination and settlement agreement.

The sole Financial Advisor on the direct listing is Maxim Group LLC.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

As of the end of 2025, AMASS had $824,962 in cash and equivalents and $25.6 million in total liabilities, of which $5.9 million was debt.

Net free cash flow for 2025 was negative $1.9 million.

Working capital at the end of 2025 was a negative $8.2 million, a weak liquidity indicator for an asset-light consumer brand company.

Also, the direct listing doesn’t provide any proceeds to repair the balance sheet and negative stockholders’ equity.

Using its trailing twelve-month operating cash flow loss, the company has a cash runway of only a few months, and its accounting firm has issued a "going concern" opinion.

AMASS Is An Unprofitable Consumer Company

AMASS has produced declining top line revenue, but improving gross profit and worsening losses.

The firm is burning cash at a quickly unsustainable rate, and a direct listing doesn’t really help the company other than providing liquidity for existing investors and perhaps enticing a potentially new investor.

While the company is trying to rationalize its portfolio mix, it is under liquidity pressure, making the listing highly speculative.

Management’s growth strategy is to expand distribution, scale its DTC efforts, launch new products and pursue acquisitions.

But where the firm is going to get the resources to pursue its goals is a mystery.

The market for its products is also under pressure, with declining usage and participation rates across younger demographics, which are the future for consumer brands.

The firm is seeking to focus more on moderation-oriented consumers, which is all to the good, but its gross profit base is too small in relation to its commercial and overhead structure.

As to risks for investors, the company is claiming "emerging growth company" and "smaller reporting company" designations, which allow management to produce substantially less information for shareholders.

Many of these company stocks have performed poorly post-IPO.

The firm also sold its AMASS trademark and it now depends on a licensed mark it doesn’t own.

AMASS also had material weaknesses in its internal control over financial reporting, namely:

Specifically, we lacked a comprehensive and formalized accounting and financial reporting policies and procedures manual addressing items such as closing procedures, descriptions of responsibilities, segregation of duties, management review procedures and the Company’s accounting policies.

(Source - SEC.)

Additionally, investor Streeterville Capital has purchased rights to Series C convertible equity, but as a variable price convertible with downside protection, protecting Streeterville but putting in place the potential for an increasing dilution effect, although it is limited to a 10% ceiling on Streeterville’s position.

My overall summary of the AMASS direct listing is:

  • Positive - Adjusting its brand portfolio strategy.

  • Uncertain - Ability to execute on growth strategies.

  • Negative - Industry decline, poor liquidity, Streeterville investment terms, material financial reporting weaknesses.

So, while management is taking steps to correct its portfolio mix, the company is operating on thin liquidity in an industry under pressure, with the only source of capital in the form of convertible financing that may have the characteristics of being increasingly dilutive to shareholders.

My outlook on the direct listing is therefore to Sell (avoid it).

Expected Direct Listing Date: To be determined.
